metrics.narrative: DuBois has never produced a quantified business case despite carrying $300-400M in combined direct and indirect spend across a 9-10 person sourcing team (Introductory Call, 3 Dec 2025). Pricing was shared as an $85K+ ballpark on that same call and a detailed module-level quote followed on 30 Jan 2026, but Tharunkumar Chandirasekaran told Zycus on 26 Feb 2026 he needs a visual, data-backed way to justify the AI spend to upper management, and that ROI has still not been built as of the last buyer contact on 26 May 2026. This is a genuine gap that will block the new cross-department budget-approval workflow Tharun described on 26 May 2026.

champion.narrative: Tharunkumar Chandirasekaran shows real champion-level depth: he drove roughly seven months of internal coordination, described himself as a 'super user' bridging sourcing, legal, and IT, and stayed responsive even while on PTO (Next Step, 11 May, 19 May 2026). However his authority is capped -- he explicitly defers budget and executive decisions to Jackie Zhang and the C-suite -- and he has gone quiet since the 26 May 2026 call describing the project as paused; no confirmed contact since, and four rep outreach attempts (11 Jun, 22 Jun, 29 Jun, 7 Jul 2026) are unanswered. This is a developing champion who is currently disengaged.

competition.narrative: Coupa (invoicing and supplier onboarding) and SAP Ariba (e-bidding) are both embedded incumbents DuBois has said it has no plans to replace (Introductory Call, 3 Dec 2025). The threat is real: Zycus's own solutions consultant told the buyer directly it 'should be natural for you to go for Coupa... they already have the supplier piece' (AI Discussion, 26 Feb 2026). DuBois's own counter was that Coupa's CLM/analytics depth 'was not as comprehensive as we were expecting' (same call), which is why Zycus remains in a roughly ten-vendor down-select whose shortlist-to-three step, due end of Q1 2026, was never completed.

identify_pain.narrative: DuBois's pain is specific and corroborated at two levels: sourcing described a rudimentary, under-leveraged contract repository driving CLM interest and reactive/manual spend reporting instead of proactive analytics (Introductory Call, 3 Dec 2025). For legal specifically, Chris Klein described spending the most time 'inserting information into the fields' manually and wanting extraction to be 'literally two clicks' (CLM Deep Dive, 11 Mar 2026). Fragmented indirect spend processes and a lack of an approved vendor list were also flagged (Discovery Session, 12 Dec 2025).

paper_process.narrative: No paper or contracting discussion has occurred in any of the seven calls read -- no MSA, SOW, or legal-redline conversation. This is expected at Formal Evaluation and not itself a red flag, but paper process is entirely unstarted.

sources: Opportunity Contact Role: Jackie Zhang, Decision Maker
economic_buyer.narrative: Jackie Zhang (VP Procurement) is named Decision Maker in the Salesforce contact roles, and Grace Kim repeatedly tried to loop her back in (Next Step notes, 17 Feb, 4 Mar, 18 Mar, tentative 24 Mar, 13 Apr, 30 Apr 2026), but none of the seven Avoma-read calls show her attending or speaking. Tharun told Grace on 11 Mar 2026 that 'she needs to give her attention more on this topic' due to the geopolitical/cost-mitigation crisis, and on 26 May 2026 confirmed the project is now a company-wide decision routed through a new cross-department approval workflow rather than sourcing alone. With no direct EB engagement recorded across seven months of pursuit, access to power is unconfirmed.

decision_process.narrative: Tharun laid out the process in detail on the 26 Feb 2026 AI call: the sourcing team owns CLM and sourcing initially, down-selecting roughly ten vendors to a top three by end of Q1 2026 (never completed due to the pause), with the legal team weighing feature fit and ease-of-use, IT (Ryan Anderson) assessing integration complexity, and the C-suite reviewing budget in parallel. Jackie Zhang (VP Procurement) reports to the COO and sits above Tharun. On 26 May 2026 Tharun confirmed a new, more formal cross-department IT-governance approval workflow now gates the project beyond the sourcing team alone.

decision_criteria.narrative: DuBois built a genuine, multi-session evaluation: Tharun compiled a feature-alignment checklist ahead of the 29 Jan 2026 demo (Next Step, 20 Jan 2026), and the legal team (Chris Klein, Laura) weighed in directly on ease-of-use and feature completeness during the 11 Mar 2026 CLM deep dive. Tharun explicitly rated legal's input as equal in weight to sourcing's ('they have an equal weightage when it comes to a decision, not more than sourcing, but equal' -- AI Discussion, 26 Feb 2026). Criteria span CLM clause-extraction accuracy (tested via a requested sandbox with real DuBois contract samples), AI/agentic capability, and bidirectional integration with Dynamics 365 and Coupa.

flags: Economic buyer Jackie Zhang (VP Procurement, Decision Maker role) has been absent since mid-April (~11 weeks) and did not attend any Q2 calls (Feb AI call, March CLM demo, May check-in). Tharun drives the evaluation but defers commercial and budget decisions to the relevant stakeholder the project restarts in Q3 Jackie must re-engage and validate the business case, or this stays a team-level productivity project that can be paused again. Recommended move: book an exec connect with Jackie in Q3 to present a quantified ROI tied to the $300–400M spend base and position Zycus as her strategic pick, not just Tharun's preference.

Zycus has not been positioned as the front-runner and has no unique wedge anchored in the buyer's mind beyond 'we saw good demos'. Recommended move: when the deal restarts, lead with the AI differentiation story (conversational analytics, autonomous negotiation, predictive sourcing) as the strategic layer above Coupa's transactional plumbing, prove seamless D365 + Coupa integration, and make the recap demo an ROI-led business case, not a feature tour, to own the top spot in the shortlist.

deltas.subject: No quantified ROI or business case. DuBois articulated pain (fragmented CLM, manual sourcing, no AI analytics) but never quantified savings, cost reduction, or cycle-time improvement across the $300–400M spend base managed by 9–10 people. Zycus shared pricing ($85K+ ballpark, detailed quote 30 Jan) but DuBois never validated value vs cost. Tharun stated on 26 May a new IT governance approval workflow is now in place and the project must pass a cross-department budget gate. Without a board-ready ROI this will not clear that gate. Recommended move: run an ROI workshop with Tharun + Jackie in Q3, quantify savings on contract cycle-time, indirect-spend consolidation, and AI-driven negotiation, and tie it to their spend base to build the business case for the IT approval workflow.

deltas.subject: Multi-vendor bake-off is frozen with no Zycus differentiation anchored. Tharun is evaluating ~10 vendors with a plan to shortlist to top 3, but the process paused in April before any finalist was named. Coupa (incumbent for onboarding + invoicing) is the embedded threat if they counter-propose a CLM or sourcing expansion. Zycus has not been positioned as the front-runner and has no unique wedge anchored in the buyer's mind beyond 'we saw good demos'. Recommended move: when the deal restarts, lead with the AI differentiation story (conversational analytics, autonomous negotiation, predictive sourcing) as the strategic layer above Coupa's transactional plumbing, prove seamless D365 + Coupa integration, and make the recap demo an ROI-led business case, not a feature tour, to own the top spot in the shortlist.
